segments.title: Bodiam Castle as display and defense
segments.summary: The book review uses Bodiam Castle to weigh late medieval castles as homes, status displays, and defenses scaled for raiding rather than major siege warfare.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke follows Mark Morris's Castle through Bodiam's famous defensive debate, then adds a practical scale argument. Features that look weak against a major siege can still make sense when the owner is a lesser noble protecting a household against raids while larger lords handle open war.

evidence.note: The review introduces the long-running debate over whether Bodiam is really a military castle and summarizes the criticisms of its walls, windows, and moat.
The transcript argues that Bodiam's elaborate approaches were meant to impress guests and that later medieval castles often had less need for serious fortification.
Dr. Clarke's own conclusion treats Bodiam as a real castle whose defenses were suited to raiding parties and household protection, not to resisting a full-scale siege.
